or **New Stone Age** (from [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") νέος *néos* 'new' and λίθος *líthos* 'stone') is an [archaeological period](/wiki/Archaeological_period "Archaeological period"), the final division of the [Stone Age](/wiki/Stone_Age "Stone Age") in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") (c. 10,000 BC to c. 2,000 BC). It saw the [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution"), a wide-ranging set of developments that appear to have arisen independently in several parts of the world. This "Neolithic package" included

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Irish_National_heritage_Park_Ferrycarrig_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3358912.jpg/250px-Irish_National_heritage_Park_Ferrycarrig_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3358912.jpg)](/wiki/File:Irish_National_heritage_Park_Ferrycarrig_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3358912.jpg)

Reconstruction of a Neolithic farmstead, [Irish National Heritage Park](/wiki/Irish_National_Heritage_Park "Irish National Heritage Park"). The Neolithic saw the [invention of agriculture](/wiki/Invention_of_agriculture "Invention of agriculture").

The **Neolithic** or **New Stone Age** (from [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") νέος *néos* 'new' and λίθος *líthos* 'stone') is an [archaeological period](/wiki/Archaeological_period "Archaeological period"), the final division of the [Stone Age](/wiki/Stone_Age "Stone Age") in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") (c. 10,000 BC to c. 2,000 BC). It saw the [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution"), a wide-ranging set of developments that appear to have arisen independently in several parts of the world. This "Neolithic package" included the [introduction of farming](/wiki/History_of_agriculture "History of agriculture"), [domestication of animals](/wiki/Domestication_of_animals "Domestication of animals"), and change from a [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") lifestyle to one of [settlement](/wiki/Sedentism "Sedentism"). The term 'Neolithic' was coined by [John Lubbock](/wiki/John_Lubbock,_1st_Baron_Avebury "John Lubbock, 1st Baron Avebury") in 1865 as a refinement of the [three-age system](/wiki/Three-age_system "Three-age system").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The Neolithic began about 12,000 years ago, when farming appeared in the [Epipalaeolithic Near East](/wiki/Epipalaeolithic_Near_East "Epipalaeolithic Near East") and [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), and later in other parts of the world. It lasted in the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East") until the transitional period of the [Chalcolithic](/wiki/Chalcolithic "Chalcolithic") (Copper Age) from about 6,500 years ago (4500 BC), marked by the development of [metallurgy](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy"), leading up to the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") and [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age").

In other places, the Neolithic followed the [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic "Mesolithic") (Middle Stone Age) and then lasted until later. In [Ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), the Neolithic lasted until the [Protodynastic period](/wiki/Protodynastic_Period_of_Egypt "Protodynastic Period of Egypt"), c. 3150 BC.[[2]](#cite_note-KSnPG-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) In [China](/wiki/China "China"), it lasted until circa 2000 BC with the rise of the [pre-Shang](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") [Erlitou culture](/wiki/Erlitou_culture "Erlitou culture"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) as it did in [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origin")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Centres_of_origin_and_spread_of_agriculture.svg/500px-Centres_of_origin_and_spread_of_agriculture.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Centres_of_origin_and_spread_of_agriculture.svg)

Approximate centers of origin of agriculture in the [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution") and its spread in prehistory: the Fertile Crescent (12,000 [BP](/wiki/Before_Present "Before Present")), the Yangtze river and Yellow River basins (9,000 BP) and the New Guinea Highlands (9,000–6,000 BP), Central Mexico (5,000–4,000 BP), Northern South America (5,000–4,000 BP), sub-Saharan Africa (5,000–4,000 BP, exact location unknown), eastern North America (4,000–3,000 BP).[[9]](#cite_note-DiamondandBellwood2003-9)

Following the [ASPRO chronology](/wiki/ASPRO_chronology "ASPRO chronology"), the Neolithic started in around 10,200 BC in the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"), arising from the [Natufian culture](/wiki/Natufian_culture "Natufian culture"), when pioneering use of wild [cereals](/wiki/Cereal "Cereal") evolved into early [farming](/wiki/Farming "Farming"). The Natufian period or "proto-Neolithic" lasted from 12,500 to 9,500 BC, and is taken to overlap with the Pre-Pottery Neolithic A (PPNA) of 10,200–8800 BC. As the Natufians had become dependent on wild cereals in their diet, and a [sedentary](/wiki/Sedentism "Sedentism") way of life had begun among them, the climatic changes associated with the [Younger Dryas](/wiki/Younger_Dryas "Younger Dryas") (about 10,000 BC) are thought to have forced people to develop farming.

The founder crops of the [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent") were [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"), [lentil](/wiki/Lentil "Lentil"), [pea](/wiki/Pea "Pea"), [chickpea](/wiki/Chickpea "Chickpea"), [bitter vetch](/wiki/Vicia_ervilia "Vicia ervilia"), and [flax](/wiki/Flax "Flax"). Among the other major crops to be domesticated were [rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice") and [millet](/wiki/Millet "Millet"). Crops were usually domesticated in a single location and ancestral wild species are still found.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Early Neolithic age farming was limited to a narrow range of plants, both wild and domesticated, which included [einkorn wheat](/wiki/Einkorn_wheat "Einkorn wheat"), [millet](/wiki/Millet "Millet") and [spelt](/wiki/Spelt "Spelt"), and the keeping of [dogs](/wiki/Origin_of_the_domestic_dog "Origin of the domestic dog"). By about 8000 BC, it included domesticated [sheep](/wiki/Sheep "Sheep") and [goats](/wiki/Goat#History "Goat"), [cattle](/wiki/Cattle "Cattle") and [pigs](/wiki/Domesticated_pig "Domesticated pig").

Not all of these cultural elements characteristic of the Neolithic appeared everywhere in the same order: the earliest farming societies in the [Near East](/wiki/Ancient_Near_East "Ancient Near East") did not use pottery. In other parts of the world, such as [Africa](/wiki/Prehistoric_North_Africa "Prehistoric North Africa"), [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asian_Stone_Age "South Asian Stone Age") and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/List_of_Neolithic_cultures_of_China "List of Neolithic cultures of China"), independent domestication events led to their own regionally distinctive Neolithic cultures, which arose completely independently of those in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [Southwest Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia"). [Early Japanese](/wiki/J%C5%8Dmon_period "Jōmon period") societies and other [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") cultures used pottery *before* developing agriculture.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Periods by region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Periods by region")]

### Near East(Western Asia)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Near East(Western Asia)")]

Main article: [Neolithic in the Near East](/wiki/Neolithic_in_the_Near_East "Neolithic in the Near East")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/N%C3%A9olithique_0001.jpg/250px-N%C3%A9olithique_0001.jpg)](/wiki/File:N%C3%A9olithique_0001.jpg)

An array of Neolithic artifacts, including bracelets, axe heads, chisels, and polishing tools

In the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), cultures identified as Neolithic began appearing in the 10th [millennium](/wiki/Millennium "Millennium") BC.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBellwood2004384-13) Early development occurred in the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") (e.g. [Pre-Pottery Neolithic A](/wiki/Pre-Pottery_Neolithic_A "Pre-Pottery Neolithic A") and [Pre-Pottery Neolithic B](/wiki/Pre-Pottery_Neolithic_B "Pre-Pottery Neolithic B")) and from there spread eastwards and westwards. Neolithic cultures are also attested in southeastern [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia"), northern [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), and the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") by around 8000 BC, reflecting the southward expansion of Pre-Pottery Neolithic traditions across the Levantine corridor.[[14]](#cite_note-Rosen2017-14)

[Anatolian Neolithic farmers](/wiki/Prehistory_of_Anatolia "Prehistory of Anatolia") derived a significant portion of their ancestry from the [Anatolian hunter-gatherers](/wiki/Anatolian_hunter-gatherers "Anatolian hunter-gatherers") (AHG), suggesting that agriculture was adopted in site by these hunter-gatherers and not spread by [demic diffusion](/wiki/Demic_diffusion "Demic diffusion") into the region.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

#### Pre-Pottery Neolithic A

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Pre-Pottery Neolithic A")]

Main article: [Pre-Pottery Neolithic A](/wiki/Pre-Pottery_Neolithic_A "Pre-Pottery Neolithic A")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Urfa_man.jpg/250px-Urfa_man.jpg)](/wiki/File:Urfa_man.jpg)

The [Urfa Man](/wiki/Urfa_Man "Urfa Man") c. 9000 BC.[[16]](#cite_note-RJC-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-AC-18) [Şanlıurfa Archaeology and Mosaic Museum](/wiki/%C5%9Eanl%C4%B1urfa_Archaeology_and_Mosaic_Museum "Şanlıurfa Archaeology and Mosaic Museum").

The Neolithic 1 (PPNA) period began around 10,000 BC in the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBellwood2004384-13) A temple area in southeastern Turkey at [Göbekli Tepe](/wiki/G%C3%B6bekli_Tepe "Göbekli Tepe"), dated to around 9500 BC, may be regarded as the beginning of the period. This site was developed by nomadic hunter-gatherer tribes, as shown by the absence of permanent housing nearby, and may be the oldest known human-made place of worship.[[19]](#cite_note-19) At least seven stone circles, covering 25 acres (10 ha), contain limestone pillars carved with animals, insects, and birds. Stone tools were used by perhaps as many as hundreds of people to create the pillars, which might have supported roofs. Other early PPNA sites dating to around 9500–9000 BC have been found in [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine"), notably in [Tell es-Sultan](/wiki/Tell_es-Sultan "Tell es-Sultan") (ancient [Jericho](/wiki/Jericho "Jericho")) and [Gilgal](/wiki/Gilgal_I "Gilgal I") in the [Jordan Valley](/wiki/Jordan_Valley_(Middle_East) "Jordan Valley (Middle East)"); [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") (notably [Ain Mallaha](/wiki/Ain_Mallaha "Ain Mallaha"), [Nahal Oren](/wiki/Nahal_Oren,_archeological_site "Nahal Oren, archeological site"), and [Kfar HaHoresh](/wiki/Kfar_HaHoresh "Kfar HaHoresh")); in [Byblos](/wiki/Byblos "Byblos"), [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"); and in [Sinai](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula"), including sites such as [Abu Madi](/wiki/Abu_Madi "Abu Madi").[[20]](#cite_note-Kuijt2000-20)

The major advance of Neolithic 1 was true farming. In the proto-Neolithic [Natufian](/wiki/Natufian "Natufian") cultures, wild cereals were harvested, and perhaps early seed selection and re-seeding occurred. The grain was ground into flour. [Emmer wheat](/wiki/Emmer_wheat "Emmer wheat") was domesticated, and animals were herded and domesticated ([animal husbandry](/wiki/Animal_husbandry "Animal husbandry") and [selective breeding](/wiki/Selective_breeding "Selective breeding")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2006, remains of [figs](/wiki/Ficus "Ficus") were discovered in a house in Jericho dated to 9400 BC. The figs are of a mutant variety that cannot be pollinated by insects, and therefore the trees can only reproduce from cuttings. This evidence suggests that figs were the first cultivated crop and mark the invention of the technology of farming. This occurred centuries before the first cultivation of grains.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Settlements became more permanent, with circular houses, much like those of the Natufians, with single rooms. However, these houses were for the first time made of [mudbrick](/wiki/Mudbrick "Mudbrick"). The settlement had a surrounding stone wall and perhaps a stone tower (as in Jericho). The wall served as protection from nearby groups, as protection from floods, or to keep animals penned. Some of the enclosures also suggest grain and meat storage.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

#### Pre-Pottery Neolithic B

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pre-Pottery Neolithic B")]

Main article: [Pre-Pottery Neolithic B](/wiki/Pre-Pottery_Neolithic_B "Pre-Pottery Neolithic B")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Neolitico_B%2C_fugurine_maschile_e_femminile%2C_da_tell_fakhariyah%2C_alabastro%2C_bitume_e_pietra%2C_9000-7000_ac_ca.jpg/250px-Neolitico_B%2C_fugurine_maschile_e_femminile%2C_da_tell_fakhariyah%2C_alabastro%2C_bitume_e_pietra%2C_9000-7000_ac_ca.jpg)](/wiki/File:Neolitico_B,_fugurine_maschile_e_femminile,_da_tell_fakhariyah,_alabastro,_bitume_e_pietra,_9000-7000_ac_ca.jpg)

Female and male figurines; 9000–7000 BC; [gypsum](/wiki/Gypsum "Gypsum") with [bitumen](/wiki/Bitumen "Bitumen") and stone inlays; from [Tell Fekheriye](/wiki/Tell_Fekheriye "Tell Fekheriye") ([Al-Hasakah Governorate](/wiki/Al-Hasakah_Governorate "Al-Hasakah Governorate") of [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria")); [University of Chicago Oriental Institute](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Oriental_Institute "University of Chicago Oriental Institute") (USA)

The Neolithic 2 (PPNB) began around 8800 BC according to the [ASPRO chronology](/wiki/ASPRO_chronology "ASPRO chronology") in the Levant ([Jericho](/wiki/Tell_es-Sultan "Tell es-Sultan"), West Bank).[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBellwood2004384-13) As with the PPNA dates, there are two versions from the same laboratories noted above. This system of terminology, however, is not convenient for southeast [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") and settlements of the middle Anatolia basin.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Studies of the Pre-Pottery Neolithic B cultural complex indicate that its long-lived and widely distributed tradition extended across the Levant from the Taurus Mountains in the north to the [Sinai](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") in the south, reflecting its broad geographic scope during this phase.[[23]](#cite_note-23) A settlement of 3,000 inhabitants called ['Ain Ghazal](/wiki/Ayn_Ghazal_(archaeological_site) "Ayn Ghazal (archaeological site)") was found in the outskirts of [Amman](/wiki/Amman "Amman"), [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"). Considered to be one of the largest prehistoric settlements in the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East"), it was continuously inhabited from approximately 7250 BC to approximately 5000 BC.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Settlements have rectangular mud-brick houses where the family lived together in single or multiple rooms. Burial findings suggest an [ancestor cult](/wiki/Ancestor_cult "Ancestor cult") where people [preserved skulls](/wiki/Plastered_human_skulls "Plastered human skulls") of the dead, which were plastered with mud to make facial features.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) The rest of the corpse could have been left outside the settlement to decay until only the bones were left, then the bones were buried inside the settlement underneath the floor or between houses.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Pre-Pottery Neolithic C

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Pre-Pottery Neolithic C")]

Main article: [Pre-Pottery Neolithic C](/wiki/Pre-Pottery_Neolithic_C "Pre-Pottery Neolithic C")

Work at the site of ['Ain Ghazal](/wiki/Ayn_Ghazal_(archaeological_site) "Ayn Ghazal (archaeological site)") in [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") has indicated a later [Pre-Pottery Neolithic C](/wiki/Pre-Pottery_Neolithic_C "Pre-Pottery Neolithic C") period. [Juris Zarins](/wiki/Juris_Zarins "Juris Zarins") has proposed that a Circum Arabian Nomadic Pastoral Complex developed in the period from the climatic crisis of 6200 BC, partly as a result of an increasing emphasis in PPNB cultures upon domesticated animals, and a fusion with [Harifian](/wiki/Harifian "Harifian") hunter gatherers in the Southern Levant, with affiliate connections with the cultures of [Fayyum](/wiki/Fayyum "Fayyum") and the [Eastern Desert](/wiki/Eastern_Desert "Eastern Desert") of [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"). Cultures practicing this lifestyle spread down the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") shoreline and moved east from [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") into southern [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

#### Late Neolithic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Late Neolithic")]

Main article: [Late Neolithic](/wiki/Late_Neolithic "Late Neolithic")

The Late Neolithic began around 6,400 BC in the [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBellwood2004384-13) By then distinctive cultures emerged, with pottery like the [Halafian](/wiki/Halafian "Halafian") (Turkey, Syria, Northern Mesopotamia) and [Ubaid](/wiki/Ubaid_period "Ubaid period") (Southern Mesopotamia). This period has been further divided into **PNA** (Pottery Neolithic A) and **PNB** (Pottery Neolithic B) at some sites.[[28]](#cite_note-28) In the southern [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev") and [Sinai](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") deserts, the Late Neolithic is characterised by the pastoralist Timnian culture, which persisted into the Bronze Age.[[29]](#cite_note-Timnian-29)

The Chalcolithic (Stone-Bronze) period began about 4500 BC, then the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") began about 3500 BC, replacing the Neolithic cultures.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Fertile Crescent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Fertile Crescent")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/20100923_amman37.JPG/250px-20100923_amman37.JPG)](/wiki/File:20100923_amman37.JPG)

['Ain Ghazal Statues](/wiki/%27Ain_Ghazal_Statues "'Ain Ghazal Statues"), found at ['Ain Ghazal](/wiki/Ayn_Ghazal_(archaeological_site) "Ayn Ghazal (archaeological site)") in [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), are considered to be one of the earliest large-scale representations of the human form dating back to around 7250 BC.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Neolithic_wall_painting_in_Tell_Bouqras%2C_Deir_ez-Zor_Museum.jpg/330px-Neolithic_wall_painting_in_Tell_Bouqras%2C_Deir_ez-Zor_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Neolithic_wall_painting_in_Tell_Bouqras,_Deir_ez-Zor_Museum.jpg)

Neolithic wall painting from [Tell Bouqras](/wiki/Tell_Bouqras "Tell Bouqras") at the [Deir ez-Zor Museum](/wiki/Deir_ez-Zor_Museum "Deir ez-Zor Museum"), Syria

Around 10,000 BC the first fully developed Neolithic cultures belonging to the phase [Pre-Pottery Neolithic A](/wiki/Pre-Pottery_Neolithic_A "Pre-Pottery Neolithic A") (PPNA) appeared in the Fertile Crescent.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBellwood2004384-13) Around 10,700–9400 BC a settlement was established in [Tell Qaramel](/wiki/Tell_Qaramel "Tell Qaramel"), 10 miles (16 km) north of [Aleppo](/wiki/Aleppo "Aleppo"). The settlement included two temples dating to 9650 BC.[[30]](#cite_note-eduskrypt-30) Around 9000 BC during the PPNA, one of the world's first towns, [Jericho](/wiki/Tell_es-Sultan "Tell es-Sultan"), appeared in the Levant. It was surrounded by a stone wall, may have contained a population of up to 2,000–3,000 people, and contained a massive stone tower.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Around 6400 BC the [Halaf culture](/wiki/Halaf_culture "Halaf culture") appeared in Syria and Northern Mesopotamia.

In 1981, a team of researchers from the [Maison de l'Orient et de la Méditerranée](/wiki/Maison_de_l%27Orient_et_de_la_M%C3%A9diterran%C3%A9e "Maison de l'Orient et de la Méditerranée"), including [Jacques Cauvin](/wiki/Jacques_Cauvin "Jacques Cauvin") and Oliver Aurenche, divided Near East Neolithic chronology into ten periods (0 to 9) based on social, economic and cultural characteristics.[[32]](#cite_note-boustani-32) In 2002, [Danielle Stordeur](/wiki/Danielle_Stordeur "Danielle Stordeur") and [Frédéric Abbès](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Abb%C3%A8s "Frédéric Abbès") advanced this system with a division into five periods.

1. [Natufian](/wiki/Natufian "Natufian") between 12,000 and 10,200 BC,
2. [Khiamian](/wiki/Khiamian "Khiamian") between 10,200 and 8800 BC, [PPNA](/wiki/PPNA "PPNA"): [Sultanian](/wiki/Sultanian "Sultanian") (Jericho), [Mureybetian](/wiki/Mureybet "Mureybet"),
3. Early PPNB (*PPNB ancien*) between 8800 and 7600 BC, middle PPNB (*PPNB moyen*) between 7600 and 6900 BC,
4. Late PPNB (*PPNB récent*) between 7500 and 7000 BC,
5. A PPNB (sometimes called PPNC) transitional stage (*PPNB final*) in which Halaf and [dark faced burnished ware](/wiki/Dark_faced_burnished_ware "Dark faced burnished ware") begin to emerge between 6900 and 6400 BC.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

They also advanced the idea of a transitional stage between the PPNA and PPNB between 8800 and 8600 BC at sites like [Jerf el Ahmar](/wiki/Jerf_el_Ahmar "Jerf el Ahmar") and [Tell Aswad](/wiki/Tell_Aswad "Tell Aswad").[[34]](#cite_note-exoriente-34)

#### Southern Mesopotamia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Southern Mesopotamia")]

Alluvial plains ([Sumer](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer")/[Elam](/wiki/Elam "Elam")). Low rainfall makes [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation") systems necessary. [Ubaid](/wiki/Ubaid_period "Ubaid period") culture originated from 6200 BC.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Northeastern Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Northeastern Africa")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/African_cave_paintings.jpg/250px-African_cave_paintings.jpg)](/wiki/File:African_cave_paintings.jpg)

Algerian cave paintings depicting hunting scenes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Megaliths_Aswan_Nubia_museum.JPG/250px-Megaliths_Aswan_Nubia_museum.JPG)](/wiki/File:Megaliths_Aswan_Nubia_museum.JPG)

Megaliths from Nabta Playa displayed in the Aswan Nubian museum

The earliest evidence of Neolithic culture in northeast Africa was found in the archaeological sites of [Bir Kiseiba](/wiki/Bir_Kiseiba "Bir Kiseiba") and [Nabta Playa](/wiki/Nabta_Playa "Nabta Playa") in what is now southwest Egypt.[[36]](#cite_note-:2-36) Domestication of [sheep](/wiki/Sheep "Sheep") and [goats](/wiki/Goats "Goats") reached [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") from the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East") possibly as early as 6000 BC.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) [Graeme Barker](/wiki/Graeme_Barker "Graeme Barker") states "The first indisputable evidence for domestic plants and animals in the Nile valley is not until the early fifth millennium BC in northern Egypt and a thousand years later further south, in both cases as part of strategies that still relied heavily on fishing, hunting, and the gathering of wild plants" and suggests that these subsistence changes were not due to farmers migrating from the Near East but was an indigenous development, with cereals either indigenous or obtained through exchange.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Other scholars argue that the primary stimulus for agriculture and domesticated animals (as well as mud-brick architecture and other Neolithic cultural features) in Egypt was from the Middle East.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### Northwestern Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Northwestern Africa")]

Main article: [Genetic history of North Africa](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_North_Africa "Genetic history of North Africa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Summary_of_inferred_population_history_of_the_Stone_Age_Maghreb.png/330px-Summary_of_inferred_population_history_of_the_Stone_Age_Maghreb.png)](/wiki/File:Summary_of_inferred_population_history_of_the_Stone_Age_Maghreb.png)

The neolithization of [Northwestern Africa](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb") corresponded to the arrival of European migration circa 5500 BC (■), and a wave of Levantine migration circa 5000 BC (■), with some local admixture (■).[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimõesGüntherMartínez-SánchezVera-Rodríguez2023554-43)

The neolithization of [Northwestern Africa](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb") was initiated by [Iberian](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula"), [Levantine](/wiki/Levant "Levant") (and perhaps [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily")) migrants around 5500–5300 BC.[[44]](#cite_note-:1-44) During the Early Neolithic period, farming was introduced by Europeans and was subsequently adopted by the locals.[[44]](#cite_note-:1-44) During the Middle Neolithic period, an influx of ancestry from the Levant appeared in Northwestern Africa, coinciding with the arrival of [pastoralism](/wiki/Pastoralism "Pastoralism") in the region.[[44]](#cite_note-:1-44) The earliest evidence for pottery, domestic cereals and [animal husbandry](/wiki/Animal_husbandry "Animal husbandry") is found in Morocco, specifically at [Kaf el-Ghar](/wiki/Kaf_El_Ghar "Kaf El Ghar").[[44]](#cite_note-:1-44)

### Sub-Saharan Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Sub-Saharan Africa")]

Further information: [Pastoral Neolithic](/wiki/Pastoral_Neolithic "Pastoral Neolithic") and [Savanna Pastoral Neolithic](/wiki/Savanna_Pastoral_Neolithic "Savanna Pastoral Neolithic")

The **Pastoral Neolithic** was a period in Africa's [prehistory](/wiki/Prehistory "Prehistory") marking the beginning of food production on the continent following the [Later Stone Age](/wiki/Later_Stone_Age "Later Stone Age"). In contrast to the Neolithic in other parts of the world, which saw the development of [farming](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") societies, the first form of African food production was mobile [pastoralism](/wiki/Pastoralism "Pastoralism"),[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) or ways of life centered on the herding and management of livestock. The term "Pastoral Neolithic" is used most often by [archaeologists](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") to describe early pastoralist periods in the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara"),[[47]](#cite_note-47) as well as in [eastern Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

The **Savanna Pastoral Neolithic** or SPN (formerly known as the **Stone Bowl Culture**) is a collection of ancient societies that appeared in the [Rift Valley](/wiki/East_African_Rift "East African Rift") of [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa") and surrounding areas during a time period known as the [Pastoral Neolithic](/wiki/Pastoral_Neolithic "Pastoral Neolithic"). They were [South Cushitic](/wiki/South_Cushitic "South Cushitic") speaking pastoralists, who tended to bury their dead in cairns whilst their toolkit was characterized by stone bowls, pestles, grindstones and earthenware pots.[[49]](#cite_note-Ambrose220-49) Through archaeology, historical linguistics and archaeogenetics, they conventionally have been identified with the area's first [Afroasiatic](/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages "Afroasiatic languages")-speaking settlers. Archaeological dating of livestock bones and burial cairns has also established the cultural complex as the earliest center of [pastoralism](/wiki/Pastoralism "Pastoralism") and stone construction in the region.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Europe")]

Main article: [Neolithic Europe](/wiki/Neolithic_Europe "Neolithic Europe")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Golemata_Majka.jpg/250px-Golemata_Majka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Golemata_Majka.jpg)

Female figure from [Tumba Madžari](/wiki/Tumba_Mad%C5%BEari "Tumba Madžari"), [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/European-late-neolithic-english.svg/250px-European-late-neolithic-english.svg.png)](/wiki/File:European-late-neolithic-english.svg)

Map showing distribution of some of the main culture complexes in [Neolithic Europe](/wiki/Neolithic_Europe "Neolithic Europe"), c. 3500 BC

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Skara_Brae_house_1_5.jpg/250px-Skara_Brae_house_1_5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skara_Brae_house_1_5.jpg)

[Skara Brae](/wiki/Skara_Brae "Skara Brae"), Scotland. Evidence of home furnishings (shelves)

In southeast [Europe](/wiki/Neolithic_Europe "Neolithic Europe") agrarian societies first appeared in the [7th millennium BC](/wiki/7th_millennium_BC "7th millennium BC"), attested by one of the earliest farming sites of Europe, discovered in [Vashtëmi](/wiki/Vasht%C3%ABmi "Vashtëmi"), southeastern [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") and dating back to 6500 BC.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) In most of Western Europe in followed over the next two thousand years, but in some parts of Northwest Europe it is much later, lasting just under 3,000 years from c. 4500 BC–1700 BC. Recent advances in [archaeogenetics](/wiki/Archaeogenetics "Archaeogenetics") have confirmed that the spread of agriculture from the Middle East to Europe was strongly correlated with the migration of [early farmers from Anatolia](/wiki/Early_European_Farmers "Early European Farmers") about 9,000 years ago, and was not just a cultural exchange.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Anthropomorphic figurines have been found in the Balkans from 6000 BC,[[55]](#cite_note-55) and in Central Europe by around 5800 BC ([La Hoguette](/wiki/La_Hoguette#Archeology "La Hoguette")). Among the earliest cultural complexes of this area are the [Sesklo](/wiki/Sesklo "Sesklo") culture in Thessaly, which later expanded in the Balkans giving rise to [Starčevo-Körös](/wiki/K%C5%91r%C3%B6s_culture "Kőrös culture") (Cris), [Linearbandkeramik](/wiki/Linearbandkeramik "Linearbandkeramik"), and [Vinča](/wiki/Vin%C4%8Da_culture "Vinča culture"). Through a combination of [cultural diffusion](/wiki/Cultural_diffusion "Cultural diffusion") and [migration of peoples](/wiki/Human_migration "Human migration"), the Neolithic traditions spread west and northwards to reach northwestern Europe by around 4500 BC. The [Vinča culture](/wiki/Vin%C4%8Da_culture "Vinča culture") may have created the earliest system of writing, the [Vinča signs](/wiki/Vin%C4%8Da_signs "Vinča signs"), though archaeologist Shan Winn believes they most likely represented [pictograms](/wiki/Pictograms "Pictograms") and [ideograms](/wiki/Ideograms "Ideograms") rather than a truly developed form of writing.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

The [Cucuteni-Trypillian culture](/wiki/Cucuteni-Trypillian_culture "Cucuteni-Trypillian culture") built enormous settlements in Romania, Moldova and Ukraine from 5300 to 2300 BC. The [megalithic](/wiki/Megalith "Megalith") temple complexes of [Ġgantija](/wiki/%C4%A0gantija "Ġgantija") on the Mediterranean island of [Gozo](/wiki/Gozo_Island "Gozo Island") (in the Maltese archipelago) and of [Mnajdra](/wiki/Mnajdra "Mnajdra") (Malta) are notable for their gigantic Neolithic structures, the oldest of which date back to around 3600 BC. The [Hypogeum of Ħal-Saflieni](/wiki/Hypogeum_of_%C4%A6al-Saflieni "Hypogeum of Ħal-Saflieni"), [Paola](/wiki/Paola,_Malta "Paola, Malta"), Malta, is a subterranean structure excavated around 2500 BC; originally a sanctuary, it became a [necropolis](/wiki/Necropolis "Necropolis"), the only prehistoric underground temple in the world, and shows a degree of artistry in stone sculpture unique in prehistory to the Maltese islands. After 2500 BC, these islands were depopulated for several decades until the arrival of a new influx of [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") immigrants, a culture that [cremated](/wiki/Cremation "Cremation") its dead and introduced smaller megalithic structures called [dolmens](/wiki/Dolmens "Dolmens") to Malta.[[57]](#cite_note-57) In most cases there are small chambers here, with the cover made of a large slab placed on upright stones. They are claimed to belong to a population different from that which built the previous megalithic temples. It is presumed the population arrived from [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") because of the similarity of Maltese dolmens to some small constructions found there.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

With some exceptions, population levels rose rapidly at the beginning of the Neolithic until they reached the [carrying capacity](/wiki/Carrying_capacity "Carrying capacity").[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShennanEdinborough2007-59) This was followed by a population crash of "enormous magnitude" after 5000 BC, with levels remaining low during the next 1,500 years.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShennanEdinborough2007-59) Populations began to rise after 3500 BC, with further dips and rises occurring between 3000 and 2500 BC but varying in date between regions.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShennanEdinborough2007-59) Around this time is the [Neolithic decline](/wiki/Neolithic_decline "Neolithic decline"), when populations collapsed across most of Europe, possibly caused by climatic conditions, plague, or mass migration.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### South and East Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: South and East Asia")]

Main articles: [Neolithic China](/wiki/Neolithic_China "Neolithic China") and [South Asian Stone Age](/wiki/South_Asian_Stone_Age "South Asian Stone Age")

Settled life, encompassing the transition from foraging to farming and pastoralism, began in South Asia in the region of [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan "Balochistan"), Pakistan, around 7,000 BC.[[61]](#cite_note-coningham-young-1-61)[[62]](#cite_note-fisher1-62)[[63]](#cite_note-dyson1-63) At the site of [Mehrgarh](/wiki/Mehrgarh "Mehrgarh"), Balochistan, presence can be documented of the domestication of wheat and barley, rapidly followed by that of goats, sheep, and cattle.[[64]](#cite_note-Wright2009-p=44-64) In April 2006, it was announced in the scientific journal *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")* that the oldest (and first *Early Neolithic*) evidence for the drilling of teeth *[in vivo](/wiki/In_vivo "In vivo")* (using [bow drills](/wiki/Bow_drill "Bow drill") and [flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint") tips) was found in Mehrgarh.[[65]](#cite_note-CoppaBondioli2006-65)

In South India, the Neolithic began by 3000 BC and lasted until around 1400 BC when the Megalithic transition period began. South Indian Neolithic is characterized by [Ash mounds](/wiki/Neolithic_ashmounds "Neolithic ashmounds") (created from ritual burning of wood, dung and animal matter) from 2500 BC in [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") region, expanded later to [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu").[[66]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Neolithic_Stone_Spades_%26_Axes.jpg/250px-Neolithic_Stone_Spades_%26_Axes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Neolithic_Stone_Spades_%26_Axes.jpg)

Neolithic artifacts from China

In East Asia, the earliest sites include the [Nanzhuangtou](/wiki/Nanzhuangtou "Nanzhuangtou") culture around 9500–9000 BC,[[67]](#cite_note-67) [Pengtoushan culture](/wiki/Pengtoushan_culture "Pengtoushan culture") around 7500–6100 BC, and [Peiligang culture](/wiki/Peiligang_culture "Peiligang culture") around 7000–5000 BC. The [prehistoric Beifudi site](/wiki/Prehistoric_Beifudi_site "Prehistoric Beifudi site") near [Yixian](/wiki/Yi_County,_Hebei "Yi County, Hebei") in Hebei Province, China, contains relics of a culture contemporaneous with the [Cishan](/wiki/Cishan_culture "Cishan culture") and [Xinglongwa](/wiki/Xinglongwa_culture "Xinglongwa culture") cultures of about 6000–5000 BC, Neolithic cultures east of the [Taihang Mountains](/wiki/Taihang_Mountains "Taihang Mountains"), filling in an archaeological gap between the two Northern Chinese cultures. The total excavated area is more than 1,200 square yards (1,000 m2; 0.10 ha), and the collection of Neolithic findings at the site encompasses two phases.[[68]](#cite_note-archdis-68) Between 3000 and 1900 BC, the [Longshan culture](/wiki/Longshan_culture "Longshan culture") existed in the middle and lower [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") valley areas of northern China. Towards the end of the 3rd millennium BC, the population decreased sharply in most of the region and many of the larger centres were abandoned, possibly due to environmental change linked to the end of the [Holocene Climatic Optimum](/wiki/Holocene_Climatic_Optimum "Holocene Climatic Optimum").[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The 'Neolithic' (defined in this paragraph as using polished stone implements) remains a living tradition in small and extremely remote and inaccessible pockets of [West Papua](/wiki/West_Papua_(region) "West Papua (region)"). Polished stone [adze](/wiki/Adzes "Adzes") and axes are used in the present day (as of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit)) in areas where the availability of metal implements is limited. This is likely to cease altogether in the next few years as the older generation die off and steel blades and chainsaws prevail.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2012, news was released about a new farming site discovered in [Munam-ri](/w/index.php?title=Munam-ri&action=edit&redlink=1 "Munam-ri (page does not exist)"), [Goseong](/wiki/Goseong,_Gangwon "Goseong, Gangwon"), [Gangwon Province](/wiki/Gangwon_Province_(South_Korea) "Gangwon Province (South Korea)"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), which may be the earliest farmland known to date in east Asia.[[70]](#cite_note-70) "No remains of an agricultural field from the Neolithic period have been found in any East Asian country before, the institute said, adding that the discovery reveals that the history of agricultural cultivation at least began during the period on the [Korean Peninsula](/wiki/Korean_Peninsula "Korean Peninsula")". The farm was dated between 3600 and 3000 BC. Pottery, stone projectile points, and possible houses were also found. "In 2002, researchers discovered prehistoric [earthenware](/wiki/Earthenware "Earthenware"), [jade](/wiki/Jade "Jade") earrings, among other items in the area". The research team will perform [accelerator mass spectrometry](/wiki/Accelerator_mass_spectrometry "Accelerator mass spectrometry") (AMS) dating to retrieve a more precise date for the site.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### The Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: The Americas")]

In [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerican_chronology "Mesoamerican chronology"), a similar set of events (i.e., crop domestication and sedentary lifestyles) occurred by around 4500 BC in South America, but possibly as early as 11,000–10,000 BC. These cultures are usually not referred to as belonging to the Neolithic; in North America, [different terms](/wiki/List_of_archaeological_periods_(North_America) "List of archaeological periods (North America)") are used such as [Formative stage](/wiki/Formative_stage "Formative stage") instead of mid-late Neolithic, [Archaic Era](/wiki/Archaic_period_in_the_Americas "Archaic period in the Americas") instead of Early Neolithic, and [Paleo-Indian](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians") for the preceding period.[[72]](#cite_note-W&P-72)

The Formative stage is equivalent to the Neolithic Revolution period in Europe, Asia, and Africa. In the southwestern United States it occurred from 500 to 1200 AD when there was a dramatic increase in population and development of large villages supported by agriculture based on [dryland farming](/wiki/Dryland_farming "Dryland farming") of corn (maize), and later, beans, squash, and domesticated turkeys. During this period the bow and arrow and ceramic pottery were also introduced.[[73]](#cite_note-NRSW-73) In later periods cities of considerable size developed, and some metallurgy by 700 BC.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Australia")]

Australia, in contrast to [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea"), has generally been held not to have had a Neolithic period, with a [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") lifestyle continuing until the arrival of Europeans. This view can be challenged in terms of the definition of agriculture, but "Neolithic" remains a rarely used and not very useful concept in discussing [Australian prehistory](/wiki/Australian_prehistory "Australian prehistory").[[75]](#cite_note-75)

## Cultural characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Cultural characteristics")]

### Social organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Social organization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Smac_Neolithikum_122.jpg/250px-Smac_Neolithikum_122.jpg)](/wiki/File:Smac_Neolithikum_122.jpg)

Model of a [Linear Pottery culture](/wiki/Linear_Pottery_culture "Linear Pottery culture") settlement, showing [longhouses](/wiki/Neolithic_long_house "Neolithic long house"), [circular enclosures](/wiki/Neolithic_circular_enclosures_in_Central_Europe "Neolithic circular enclosures in Central Europe"), and fields

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/MotherGoddessFertility.JPG/250px-MotherGoddessFertility.JPG)](/wiki/File:MotherGoddessFertility.JPG)

Anthropomorphic Neolithic ceramic figurine

During most of the Neolithic age of [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia"), people lived in small [tribes](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe") composed of multiple bands or lineages.[[76]](#cite_note-Leonard_D._Katz_Rigby_2000_352-76) There is little [scientific evidence](/wiki/Scientific_evidence "Scientific evidence") of developed [social stratification](/wiki/Social_stratification "Social stratification") in most Neolithic societies; social stratification is more associated with the later [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age").[[77]](#cite_note-77) Although some late Eurasian Neolithic societies formed complex stratified chiefdoms or even [states](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"), generally states evolved in Eurasia only with the rise of metallurgy, and most Neolithic societies on the whole were relatively simple and egalitarian.[[76]](#cite_note-Leonard_D._Katz_Rigby_2000_352-76) Beyond Eurasia, however, states were formed during the local Neolithic in three areas, namely in the [Preceramic Andes](/wiki/Cultural_periods_of_Peru "Cultural periods of Peru") with the [Caral-Supe Civilization](/wiki/Caral%E2%80%93Supe_civilization "Caral–Supe civilization"),[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) [Formative Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerican_chronology "Mesoamerican chronology") and [Ancient Hawaiʻi](/wiki/Ancient_Hawaii "Ancient Hawaii").[[80]](#cite_note-80) However, most Neolithic societies were noticeably more hierarchical than the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic") cultures that preceded them and hunter-gatherer cultures in general.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-b1-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Clay_human_figurine_%28Fertility_goddess%29_Tappeh_Sarab%2C_Kermanshah_ca._7000-6100_BCE_Neolithic_period%2C_National_Museum_of_Iran.jpg/250px-Clay_human_figurine_%28Fertility_goddess%29_Tappeh_Sarab%2C_Kermanshah_ca._7000-6100_BCE_Neolithic_period%2C_National_Museum_of_Iran.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clay_human_figurine_(Fertility_goddess)_Tappeh_Sarab,_Kermanshah_ca._7000-6100_BCE_Neolithic_period,_National_Museum_of_Iran.jpg)

Clay human figurine (Fertility goddess) Tappeh Sarab, Kermanshah c. 7000–6100 BC, National Museum of Iran

The [domestication](/wiki/Domestication_of_animals "Domestication of animals") of [large animals](/wiki/Megafauna "Megafauna") (c. 8000 BC) resulted in a dramatic increase in social inequality in most of the areas where it occurred; [New Guinea](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Papua_New_Guinea "Agriculture in Papua New Guinea") being a notable exception.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Possession of livestock allowed competition between households and resulted in inherited inequalities of wealth. Neolithic pastoralists who controlled large herds gradually acquired more livestock, and this made economic inequalities more pronounced.[[84]](#cite_note-Bahn,_Paul_1996-84) However, evidence of social inequality is still disputed, as settlements such as [Çatalhöyük](/wiki/%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Çatalhöyük") reveal a lack of difference in the size of homes and burial sites, suggesting a more egalitarian society with no evidence of the concept of capital, although some homes do appear slightly larger or more elaborately decorated than others.[[85]](#cite_note-Bogaard_et_al-85)

Families and households were still largely independent economically, and the household was probably the center of life.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87) However, excavations in [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") have revealed that early Neolithic [Linear Ceramic cultures](/wiki/Linear_Ceramic_culture "Linear Ceramic culture") ("*Linearbandkeramik*") were building large arrangements of [circular ditches](/wiki/Circular_ditches "Circular ditches") between 4800 and 4600 BC. These structures (and their later counterparts such as [causewayed enclosures](/wiki/Causewayed_enclosure "Causewayed enclosure"), [burial mounds](/wiki/Burial_mound "Burial mound"), and [henge](/wiki/Henges "Henges")) required considerable time and labour to construct, which suggests that some influential individuals were able to organise and direct human labour – though non-hierarchical and voluntary work remain possibilities.

There is a large body of evidence for fortified settlements at *Linearbandkeramik* sites along the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"), as at least some villages were fortified for some time with a [palisade](/wiki/Palisade "Palisade") and an outer ditch.[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) Settlements with palisades and weapon-traumatized bones, such as those found at the [Talheim Death Pit](/wiki/Talheim_Death_Pit "Talheim Death Pit"), have been discovered and demonstrate that "...systematic violence between groups" and [warfare](/wiki/Prehistoric_warfare "Prehistoric warfare") was probably much more common during the Neolithic than in the preceding Paleolithic period.[[82]](#cite_note-b1-82) This supplanted an earlier view of the [Linear Pottery Culture](/wiki/Linear_Pottery_culture "Linear Pottery culture") as living a "peaceful, unfortified lifestyle".[[90]](#cite_note-90) Violence increased toward the end of this culture which existed at 5500–4500 BC.[[91]](#cite_note-91) In 2024, a study suggested a peaceful explanation to the reduction in the size of male population observed worldwide 5000–3000 years ago.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

Control of labour and inter-group conflict is characteristic of [tribal](/wiki/Tribal "Tribal") groups with [social rank](/wiki/Social_rank "Social rank") that are headed by a charismatic individual – either a '[big man](/wiki/Big_man_(anthropology) "Big man (anthropology)")' or a proto-[chief](/wiki/Tribal_chief "Tribal chief") – functioning as a lineage-group head. Whether a non-hierarchical system of organization existed is debatable, and there is no evidence that explicitly suggests that Neolithic societies functioned under any dominating class or individual, as was the case in the [chiefdoms](/wiki/Chiefdom "Chiefdom") of the European [Early Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age").[[93]](#cite_note-93) Possible exceptions to this include Iraq during the [Ubaid period](/wiki/Ubaid_period "Ubaid period") and England beginning in the Early Neolithic (4100–3000 BC).[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) Theories to explain the apparent implied egalitarianism of Neolithic (and Paleolithic) societies have arisen, notably the [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") concept of [primitive communism](/wiki/Primitive_communism "Primitive communism").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Phylogenies reconstructed from modern genetic data indicates an extreme drop in Y-chromosomal diversity occurred during the Neolithic, with [effective population size](/wiki/Effective_population_size "Effective population size") for the mitochondria up to 17 times higher than for the Y-chromosomes during this period.[[96]](#cite_note-96) The causes of this [bottleneck](/wiki/Neolithic_Y-chromosome_bottleneck "Neolithic Y-chromosome bottleneck") remain poorly understood. At a basic level, it can likely be attributed to a culture-induced change in the distribution of male reproductive success, with possible explanations ranging from an increased incidence of violence and male mortality during the Neolithic[[97]](#cite_note-97) to the rise of patrilineal segmentary groups with varying reproductive success due to polygyny.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

### Shelter and sedentism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Shelter and sedentism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Neolithic_house.JPG/250px-Neolithic_house.JPG)](/wiki/File:Neolithic_house.JPG)

Reconstruction of Neolithic house in [Tuzla](/wiki/Tuzla "Tuzla"), [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina")

See also: [Neolithic architecture](/wiki/Neolithic_architecture "Neolithic architecture") and [History of construction](/wiki/History_of_construction "History of construction")

The shelter of early people changed dramatically from the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic") to the Neolithic era. In the Paleolithic, people did not normally live in permanent constructions. In the Neolithic, mud brick houses started appearing that were coated with plaster.[[99]](#cite_note-firstcity-99) This increased use of clay for building, along with the development of pottery and other clay-based artifacts, has led some to refer to the Neolithic period as the **Age of Clay**.[[100]](#cite_note-f818-100) The growth of agriculture made permanent houses far more common. At [Çatalhöyük](/wiki/%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Çatalhöyük") 9,000 years ago, doorways were made on the roof, with ladders positioned both on the inside and outside of the houses.[[99]](#cite_note-firstcity-99) [Stilt-house](/wiki/Stilt-houses "Stilt-houses") settlements were common in the [Alpine](/wiki/Alps "Alps") and [Pianura Padana](/wiki/Pianura_Padana "Pianura Padana") ([Terramare](/wiki/Terramare "Terramare")) region.[[101]](#cite_note-Ertl2008-101) Remains have been found in the [Ljubljana Marsh](/wiki/Ljubljana_Marsh "Ljubljana Marsh") in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") and at the [Mondsee](/wiki/Mondsee_(lake) "Mondsee (lake)") and [Attersee](/wiki/Attersee_(lake) "Attersee (lake)") lakes in [Upper Austria](/wiki/Upper_Austria "Upper Austria"), for example.

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

Main article: [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/CucuteniAgriculture.JPG/250px-CucuteniAgriculture.JPG)](/wiki/File:CucuteniAgriculture.JPG)

A [Cucuteni-Trypillian culture](/wiki/Cucuteni-Trypillian_culture "Cucuteni-Trypillian culture") [deer antler](/wiki/Antler "Antler") [plough](/wiki/Ard_(plough) "Ard (plough)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/HMB_Essen_und_Kochger%C3%A4t_Jungsteinzeit.jpg/250px-HMB_Essen_und_Kochger%C3%A4t_Jungsteinzeit.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMB_Essen_und_Kochger%C3%A4t_Jungsteinzeit.jpg)

Food and cooking items retrieved at a European Neolithic site: [millstones](/wiki/Millstone "Millstone"), charred bread, grains and small apples, a clay cooking pot, and containers made of antlers and wood

A significant and far-reaching shift in human [subsistence](/wiki/Subsistence "Subsistence") and lifestyle was to be brought about in areas where crop [farming](/wiki/Farm "Farm") and cultivation were first developed: the previous reliance on an essentially [nomadic](/wiki/Nomad "Nomad") [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") [subsistence technique](/wiki/List_of_subsistence_techniques "List of subsistence techniques") or [pastoral transhumance](/wiki/Transhumance "Transhumance") was at first supplemented, and then increasingly replaced by, a reliance upon the foods produced from cultivated lands. These developments are also believed to have greatly encouraged the growth of settlements, since it may be supposed that the increased need to spend more time and labor in tending crop fields required more localized dwellings. This trend would continue into the Bronze Age, eventually giving rise to permanently settled farming [towns](/wiki/Town "Town"), and later [cities](/wiki/City "City") and [states](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") whose larger populations could be sustained by the increased productivity from cultivated lands.

The profound differences in human interactions and subsistence methods associated with the onset of early agricultural practices in the Neolithic have been called the *[Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution")*, a term [coined](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") in the 1920s by the Australian archaeologist [Vere Gordon Childe](/wiki/Vere_Gordon_Childe "Vere Gordon Childe").

One potential benefit of the development and increasing sophistication of farming technology was the possibility of producing surplus crop yields, in other words, food supplies in excess of the immediate needs of the community. Surpluses could be stored for later use, or possibly traded for other necessities or luxuries. Agricultural life afforded securities that nomadic life could not, and sedentary farming populations grew faster than nomadic.

However, early farmers were also adversely affected in times of [famine](/wiki/Famine "Famine"), such as may be caused by [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought") or [pests](/wiki/Pest_control "Pest control"). In instances where agriculture had become the predominant way of life, the sensitivity to these shortages could be particularly acute, affecting agrarian populations to an extent that otherwise may not have been routinely experienced by prior hunter-gatherer communities.[[84]](#cite_note-Bahn,_Paul_1996-84) Nevertheless, agrarian communities generally proved successful, and their growth and the expansion of territory under cultivation continued.

Another significant change undergone by many of these newly agrarian communities was one of [diet](/wiki/Diet_(nutrition) "Diet (nutrition)"). Pre-agrarian diets varied by region, season, available local plant and animal resources and degree of pastoralism and hunting. Post-agrarian diet was restricted to a limited package of successfully cultivated cereal grains, plants and to a variable extent domesticated animals and animal products. Supplementation of diet by hunting and gathering was to variable degrees precluded by the increase in population above the carrying capacity of the land and a high sedentary local population concentration. In some cultures, there would have been a significant shift toward increased starch and plant protein. The relative nutritional benefits and drawbacks of these dietary changes and their overall impact on early societal development are still debated.

In addition, increased population density, decreased population mobility, increased continuous proximity to domesticated animals, and continuous occupation of comparatively population-dense sites would have altered [sanitation](/wiki/Sanitation "Sanitation") needs and patterns of [disease](/wiki/Disease "Disease").

### Lithic technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Lithic technology")]

Main article: [Stone tool § Neolithic industries](/wiki/Stone_tool#Neolithic_industries "Stone tool")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Neolithic "Special:EditPage/Neolithic") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(April 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The identifying characteristic of Neolithic technology is the use of polished or ground stone tools, in contrast to the flaked stone tools used during the Paleolithic era.

Neolithic people were skilled farmers, manufacturing a range of tools necessary for the tending, harvesting and processing of crops (such as [sickle](/wiki/Sickle "Sickle") blades and [grinding stones](/wiki/Grinding_stone "Grinding stone")) and food production (e.g. [pottery](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery"), bone implements). They were also skilled manufacturers of a range of other types of stone tools and ornaments, including [projectile points](/wiki/Projectile_point "Projectile point"), [beads](/wiki/Bead "Bead"), and [statuettes](/wiki/Statuette "Statuette"). But what allowed forest clearance on a large scale was the polished [stone axe](/wiki/Stone_axe "Stone axe") above all other tools. Together with the [adze](/wiki/Adze "Adze"), fashioning wood for shelter, structures and [canoes](/wiki/Canoe "Canoe") for example, this enabled them to exploit the newly developed farmland.

Neolithic peoples in the Levant, Anatolia, Syria, northern Mesopotamia and [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") were also accomplished builders, utilizing mud-brick to construct houses and villages. At [Çatalhöyük](/wiki/%C3%87atalh%C3%B6y%C3%BCk "Çatalhöyük"), houses were [plastered](/wiki/Plaster "Plaster") and painted with elaborate scenes of humans and animals. In [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [long houses](/wiki/Neolithic_long_house "Neolithic long house") built from [wattle and daub](/wiki/Wattle_and_daub "Wattle and daub") were constructed. Elaborate [tombs](/wiki/Tomb "Tomb") were built for the dead. These tombs are particularly numerous in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), where there are many thousand still in existence. Neolithic people in the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") built [long barrows](/wiki/Long_barrow "Long barrow") and [chamber tombs](/wiki/Chamber_tomb "Chamber tomb") for their dead and [causewayed camps](/wiki/Causewayed_camp "Causewayed camp"), henges, flint mines and [cursus](/wiki/Cursus "Cursus") monuments. It was also important to figure out ways of preserving food for future months, such as fashioning relatively airtight containers, and using substances like [salt](/wiki/Salt "Salt") as preservatives.

The peoples of the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") and the [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific "Pacific") mostly retained the Neolithic level of tool [technology](/wiki/Technology "Technology") until the time of European contact. Exceptions include copper [hatchets](/wiki/Hatchet "Hatchet") and [spearheads](/wiki/Spear "Spear") in the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes_(North_America) "Great Lakes (North America)") region.

### Clothing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neolithic&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Clothing")]

Most clothing appears to have been made of animal skins, as indicated by finds of large numbers of bone and antler pins that are ideal for fastening leather. [Wool](/wiki/Woolen "Woolen") cloth and [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen") might have become available during the later Neolithic,[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103) as suggested by finds of perforated stones that (depending on size) may have served as [spindle whorls](/wiki/Spindle_(textiles) "Spindle (textiles)") or [loom](/wiki/Loom "Loom") weights.[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106)
